back: Remember, model development is just the beginning. Understanding the full lifecycle, from data preparation to deployment, is crucial for successful machine learning projects.
source_chunk:
[Deep Learning with Python, Third Edition > Deploying your model > Maintaining your model]
This concludes the universal workflow of machine learning — that’s a lot of things to keep in mind. It takes time and experience to become an expert, but don’t worry, you’re already a lot wiser than you were a few chapters ago. You are now familiar with the big picture — the entire spectrum of what machine learning projects entail. While most of this book will focus on the model development part, you’re now aware that it’s only one part of the entire workflow. Always keep in mind the big picture!

back: In ConvNets, features from earlier layers detect simple shapes, while deeper layers form complex object representations. Lateral connections enhance this by integrating lower-level and higher-level features.

different types of simple features like edges and curves, but only later in the
ConvNet layers do we start building latent representations for entire objects.  
The solution used by RetinaNet is called a feature pyramid network. The final
features from the ConvNet base model are upsampled with progressive
`Conv2DTranspose`  
layers, just like we saw in the previous chapter. But critically, we also
include *lateral connections* where we sum these upsampled feature maps with the

back: Keras abstracts away the backend details, allowing users to focus on building models. Works with TensorFlow, PyTorch, or JAX. Central class is Layer.
topic: Chapter 3: Introduction to TensorFlow, PyTorch, JAX, and Keras
source_chunk:
[Deep Learning with Python > Chapter 3: Introduction to TensorFlow, PyTorch, JAX, and Keras > Summary]
## Summary  
- TensorFlow, PyTorch, and JAX are three popular low-level frameworks for numerical computation and autodifferentiation. They all have their own way of doing things and their own strengths and weaknesses.
- Keras is a high-level API for building and training neural networks. It can be used with either TensorFlow, PyTorch, or JAX — just pick the backend you like best.
- The central class of Keras is the
`Layer`  
. A layer encapsulates some weights and some computation. Layers are

front: RNN processes sequences using state info
back: An RNN maintains internal state as it iterates over sequence elements, allowing it to consider past inputs when processing current ones.
source_chunk:
Source: Deep Learning with Python > Chapter 13: Timeseries forecasting > Recurrent neural networks > Understanding recurrent neural networks

A *recurrent neural network* (RNN) adopts the same principle,
albeit in an extremely simplified version: it
processes sequences by iterating through the sequence elements and maintaining
a *state* containing information relative to what it has seen so far. In
effect, an RNN is a type of neural network that has an internal *loop* (see
figure 13.6).

---

Source: Deep Learning with Python > Chapter 13: Timeseries forecasting > Different kinds of timeseries

front: Use recurrent neural networks for time series
back: RNNs preserve the temporal structure of data; flatten or pool for other models.
source_chunk:
Source: Deep Learning with Python > Chapter 13: Timeseries forecasting > Recurrent neural networks

Neither the fully connected approach nor the convolutional approach did well, but that doesn’t mean machine learning isn’t applicable to this problem. The densely connected approach first flattened the timeseries, which removed the notion of time from the input data. The convolutional approach treated every segment of the data in the same way, even applying pooling, which destroyed order information. Let’s instead look at the data as what it is: a sequence, where causality and order matter.

Let’s

front: Carrying info across timesteps in Keras
back: A recurrent layer allows sequences to pass information between time steps via a 'conveyor belt', enabling long-term memory similar to LSTM. This concept resembles residual connections.
source_chunk:
Source: Deep Learning with Python > Chapter 13: Timeseries forecasting > Recurrent neural networks > A recurrent layer in Keras

adds a way to carry information across many timesteps. Imagine a conveyor belt
running parallel to the sequence you’re processing. Information from the
sequence can jump onto the conveyor belt at any point, be transported to a
later timestep, and jump off, intact, when you need it. This is essentially
what LSTM does: it saves information for later, thus preventing older signals
from gradually vanishing during processing.
